explanation: -On the sessions that I used Forest to blacklist specific websites, I wrote more measures than session where I didn’t restrict distractions -On session 3, I felt stuck on writing, so I ended up just browsing youtube and messaging on Discord, only writing 6 measures -Forest was noticeably helpful, as sometimes I would subconsciously go on a blacklisted site and the extension would overlay on the screen to remind me of the time

Jeffrey - Reflection for Week 1 | Only two sessions per week, One topic per week. 200 minutes per session. This week was focused on Perspective. Each session was recorded with a 70 point rubric Quality(30 points) Teachings applied (10 points) Work Completed (10 points) Neatness (10 points) Satisfaction(30 points) Engagement (10 points) Satisfaction with result (10 points) Feeling of Improvement (10 points) Information Retained(10 points) 10 question quiz (10 points) | The overall score was tied at 61 While the time saving features of digital were helpful, I still feel that I enjoyed traditional drawing more.

-For this week, I’ve noticed that the difference in performance between using and not using Forest when writing is not as dramatic as least week, but still very effective -For the sessions that I did not restrict distractions, I spent a non-negligible amount of time unfocused instead of writing, just like last week -A total of 4hrs 10mins was spent this week, which is down 40 min from last week

Jeffrey - Reflection for Week 3 | Felt that results were similar, but the process of drawing digitally was more forgiving. The digital drawing process allowed for more mistakes to be made, and easily remedied than with traditional. Some notable examples of how it was ‘more forgiving’ include the eraser being less messy, allowing me to undo mistakes more easily. Along with the resize tool allowing me to fix proportions if needed. This was especially helpful for this week. The difficulty of anatomy led to me requiring to redo some aspects many times. | Increased the time to 160 minutes for this week. Chose to study Anatomy. Did digital on Monday, and traditional on wednesday this week. Felt that that anatomy would be more difficult, and demanded more time to study. Overall Scores: 64/70 on Digital 60/70 on Traditional

Found traditional to be much more satisfying for shading. Often times during the digital session, I forgot about the digital features and instead just shaded as if I was using traditional. Adding each individual line on top of each other felt like I was slowly yet surely building up to something. | Gave myself 120 minutes for this week. Chose to study shading. Did traditional on Monday, and digital on wednesday this week. Felt that I had already studied some of this while learning textures, so I allocated less time. Overall Scores 62/70 on traditional 62/70 on digital

So what does this mean? Despite the many improvements and quality of life features provided by digital art, it still managed to produce overall results on par with or similar to the traditional sessions. | Sequential plot of overall scores Inconsistent when sorting by medium Noticeable spike in between Day 2(Sessions 3 and 4) and Day 4(Sessions 5 and 6). | Overall scores for each week 2 weeks tied Week 3 was better for digital Week 2 was better for traditional

So what does this mean? Despite the many improvements and quality of life features provided by digital art, it still managed to produce overall results on par with or similar to the traditional sessions. | Sequential plot of overall scores Inconsistent when sorting by medium Noticeable spike in between Day 2(Sessions 3 and 4) and Day 4(Sessions 5 and 6). | Overall scores for each week 2 weeks tied Week 3 was better for digital Week 2 was better for traditional

Even quantitative data, such as the information retained, did not provide much correlation between the two mediums. 1 week tied. Digital performed better on 1 week. Traditional performed better on 2 weeks. While there is evidence that traditional performed better, it’s not significant enough to draw conclusions with. There is an argument that the lower scores could be due to not understanding the concept, and not the medium used.

-The bar chart shows the total measures per track completed for Treatment and No treatment -More than double the measures were written while using Forest vs not using forest (session # of each were the same at 8)

-This line chart displays the time I was on something else instead of writing for the sessions that I didn’t use Forest for. -There is a rough trend of spending less time distracted as the sessions went on throughout the 4 weeks. -I felt that Forest has influenced sessions even when I wasn’t using it, building a habit of not opening irrelevant stuff like social media

-This graph shows that all 8 sessions using Forest were passed (did not visit any of the blacklisted websites) -I initially felt I should record this column because I believed I might fail some sessions, but the app’s reminder overlay made it easy to succeed

-I placed a requirement during these weeks that no session would be less than an hour (all no treatment sessions were an hour exactly) -A couple sessions early on using forest would sometimes be longer than 60 minutes, but then decreased to just an hour towards the end -It was most likely the initial motivation of using Forest that made me spend more time at first, but made no difference in the end compared to not using it
